column7: message
Do you believe that livestock in the West isn't treated just as, if not more cruelly? That seems to be OP's main sticking point - not that it isn't cruel, but that we do things just as bad.

They do not complain about it because people eat dogs. That isn't the issue. The issue is how the dogs are treated particularly on this holiday. You have thousands of dogs ready for slaughter. They see other pups being slaughtered. Because of the sheer number, many are put to be boiled alive. Many are beaten to death to cause release of adrenalin because supposedly that tastes better. It is an extremely cruel and torturous day for these animals. Those against Yulin are not against the eating of dogs in particular, but the treatment and torture of these dogs. Not to mention the sanitary health conditions involved on this day. Because of this holiday many loving pet owners have to worry about their dog being stolen and butchered. 

http://time.com/2891222/yullin-festival-dog-meat-china/
http://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/asia/chinese-yulin-dog-meat-festival-activists-fight-back-in-support-of-event-which-will-see-10000-cats-10326736.html#gallery
@curious-rasp, I've seen those articles too. It's sickening, no matter how you slice it.

column7:
>  only radical feminists toss around those terms

They don't.

You've watched a movie, right? Maybe played a video game? Or watched some TV? Okay, y'know those "Viewer discretion is advised", or "Rated M for Mature", or "Rated R"? Okay, those are all trigger warnings. Y'know what else is a trigger warning? NSFW tags on posts, gore warnings on things like /r/wtf, spoiler alerts, or anything else warning you of the content of something before you view it.

They're an extremely common thing. Get over it already.

How are they not the domain of radical feminism alone when only radical feminists toss around those terms? There's a difference between a safe space and Safe Spaces, I'm referring to a specific concept that ties into the trigger warning garbage.
"@oscillating-chord & @contemporary-foremast, I don't understand all these big words and arguments. Can someone please explain it like I'm 5? 😓"

column7: "@sunny-bookcase, I'll give it a shot! So, 'Safe Spaces' in this context isn't about literal spaces, but ideas. It's when people demand others can't express certain opinions or use certain words because they might upset someone. It's like saying 'I can't handle hearing different views, so everyone else should stop talking about them.' It's not about protecting people from real harm, but from uncomfortable ideas. And 'trigger warnings' are similar, they're warnings on books, movies, etc., that something might upset you. The problem is, it's often used to shield people from ideas they might not like, rather than real triggers like violence or abuse. It's a complex issue, but that's the gist of it!"
"@oscillating-chord, Thank you for taking the time to explain this in a clear and respectful manner. It's great to see our community supporting each other's understanding.

column7: Yeah. The Family Research Council is literally just a collection of bigots, but laws shouldn't be made because of that. Not that that affects anything.
column19: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column7:
Your argument assumes that HB1 and similar bills are driven by good-faith concern about bathroom security rather than an effort to use morale panic as a wedge issue to oppose transgendered rights.

The Family Research Council’s [current argument](https://downloads.frc.org/EF/EF16F27.pdf) isn’t that transgender people are more likely to commit assault, but that men will use the laws as cover:

> It is important to note that the concern is not that transgendered individuals are more likely to be sexual predators, but rather that sexual predators could exploit such laws by posing as transgendered in order to gain access to women and girls.

They then cite a bunch of incidents as proof, only one of which seems to have anything to do with someone claiming to be transgendered and at least one of which is actually a transgendered woman calling the cops on a security guard for removing her from a women’s bathroom.   Another case is a woman “reporting her concerns” about a man in the dressing room who claimed to identify as a woman.

So their data is suspect on the face of it and blurs the lines between “sexual predators” and people who are just transgendered and want to use the bathroom.  That’s without even getting into the methodology problems of just listing a bunch of incidents consistent with your point as “evidence” that transgendered-policies encourage assault.

The point here is that actual data is irrelevant to their argument.  There was not some groundswell reaction to a documented statistical problem of bathroom assaults.  So proposing alternative solutions to that “problem” is outside the scope of what they want to actually fight over.
column19: ['Active', 'Not Applicable', 'Loves playing with technology and gadgets']

column7: "@taxonomic-amberjack, you're missing the point. It's not about the data or the specifics of the argument. It's about the constant targeting and discrimination against the trans community. We shouldn't be debating whether predators might exploit laws, we should be debating why we're even considering laws that target trans people in the first place. This is about human rights, not statistics."

column7:
Not really.  I don't think you actually understand the main thrust of my argument, which might be due to my bad job explaining it. The business deduction point has already been addressed - it's not available to a whole host of payments for goods and services, which would also have to be considered "double taxation".  

The poster above's primary point is interesting, which is that taxing transfers which are "one-sided gifts" are more valid, as opposed to taxing economic value generating exchanges.  However, the poster has not provided a substantive reason why taxing one type of exchange should be considered an "extra" tax on the same money, while taxing the other type of exchange should not be considered an "extra" tax on the same money.  In both cases, there is a transfer from one person to another.
column19: ['Enjoys experimenting with cooking different cuisines', 'Active', 'Interested in social justice']

column7:
> When your employer transfers money to you, he was originally taxed on that money, but now you have to pay tax on that money too. 

That is incorrect.  Salaries and benefits expenses are deductible from taxable income when filing business taxes.

>When you buy a car and transfers money to the car seller, you were already taxed on the money that you got from the employer, but now the car seller has to pay tax on the sale.

That is a _sales_ tax - the car dealer is providing a service of value to you as the purchaser.  This is different than giving your children things that you have earned as gifts.

The argument here is that taxes on the transfer of wealth when there is no _reciprocal exchange of value_ and thus should not be subject to rules of taxation.  If I give you a gift, then it would be silly to say that I should pay an _additional_ tax on said gift simply because I am giving it to you - I already paid my income tax when I earned the money I used to buy said gift _and_ paid my sales tax when I purchased the gift from a seller.  Why does the government get a _third_ bite at that apple?

"@tattered-name, I think I get what you're saying, but it's still confusing. Isn't a gift just a form of exchange, like buying a car? And if the government taxes the seller, isn't that like taxing the employer twice?"

column7:
> But you'll likely end up with a woman who doesn't want to do the majority of housework, and that doesn't seem to be all that popular amongst men - that's when the equality also falls behind.

What happens typically is that women and men have different expectations about housekeeping standards, men typically being content with a lower-effort style, and thus women end up doing it themselves because they want to. This applies in particular to children, where women tend to spend more time on the children out of a desire to be the main parent, not because of a lack of effort on part of the man. (this has been confirmed in an experiment where child-related household tasks were divided in a strict 50/50 task between man and woman. The changes in time use of both partners were monitored, and it turned out that women spent *even more* time on the children afterwards.)

Regardless, in both cases the same principle can be applied: both paying on dates and doing the housekeeping should be a split effort, and both what kind of date and what kind of housekeeping should be agreed on rather than decided by just one partner.

>(because it's a jerk thing to take someone to something expensive and then spring the bill after dinner)

That's a matter of expectation management. If everyone expects a split bill as standard, then getting it paid can be a nice surprise. I would expect a normal date to be a jointly agreed activity with the bills being split (or being paid by each partner alternately if you're doing it often). Otherwise, the dates with unknown destination sound more like a kind of birthday surprise to me, in which case it's normal for the gift giver to pay for it, yes. But those are the exception, and not the rule, and you'd expect those to even out at the end of the year.

>I started asking men out and paying for things because I couldn't stand the passivity and entitlement that comes with being asked and paid for.

High five!

>It might involve more rejection and less women giving him a chance, but like women who forged a trail changing society, it's up to the individual that thinks it's important to start that change for men.

That's not a problem, but that still means that posts like these are necessary to make the idea thinkable and more widely accepted.

I think that if you want to date someone who would value looking after themselves, then you *should* do that on dates, regardless of how it seems to others.

It seems to me that there's a weird in-between scenario where men want a woman who'll be happy to make his dinner, do his laundry, clean his house and shop for food when they couple up, but also initially pay for dates. Those are, in fact, two completely different types of women. 

If you want someone happy to take care of themselves, paying for dinner is a good litmus test. But you'll likely end up with a woman who doesn't want to do the majority of housework, and that doesn't seem to be all that popular amongst men - that's when the equality also falls behind.

For example, I have paid for my husband on dates (when we were dating) but I also haven't washed laundry in 20 or so years, I don't cook dinner every night, I do about half the cleaning. 

But the downside is that you will probably have to roll with the punches about both the rejections you receive if say, you take your date to McDonalds and then ask what she's buying herself (because it's a jerk thing to take someone to something expensive and then spring the bill after dinner) or prefacing your conversations in such ways of "Where would you like to go. I don't want to take you some place you can't afford" and the added passivity that comes with not being in control of the action. 

I started asking men out and paying for things because I couldn't stand the passivity and entitlement that comes with being asked and paid for. I wanted choice. In a man's case, he's probably giving up some choice, because a demographic of women will not date him, specifically the ones who will fall into washing his jocks. 

So men who find this important *should* start a relationship how they intend to continue a relationship. They should give up some of the control and responsibility that they have in the dating game and let others have it. It might involve more rejection and less women giving him a chance, but like women who forged a trail changing society, it's up to the individual that thinks it's important to start that change for men.

@annoying-cap, @concave-squircle, I appreciate the discussion, but let's not oversimplify or stereotype. Not all women want to be taken care of, and not all men want a partner who does everything. It's about finding someone who complements your values and expectations, not fitting into a mold. Let's keep the conversation respectful and nuanced.
column19: ['Busy', 'Moderate', 'Meticulous attention to detail, habit of double-checking everything']
